coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive fluorometric determination of oxytetracycline based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 MNPs
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yilin
Ni, Pengjuan
Jiang, Shu
Lu, Wangdong
Li, Zhen
Liu, Hanmeng
Lin, Jing
Sun, Yujing
Li, Zhuang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel, selective and eco-friendly biosensor has been fabricated and applied for sensitive detection of antibiotic oxytetracycline (OTC). This fluorescent biosensor was based on carbon quantum dots (CDs), H2O2 and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs). Herein, we firstly demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of OTC, H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs. The fluorescence quenching of CDs not only is caused by the hydroxyl radical produced from the Fenton reaction and complexation of Fe3O4 MNPs with OTC, but also might be related to the inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and OTC, which improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the fabricated biosensor. Under the optimal experiment conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased linearly with the increasing concentration of OTC from 25nM to 1.75μM. The detection limit of the fabricated biosensor on OTC was 9.5nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Compared with other current protocols, the method described here displayed a few advantages including simplicity, low cost and rapid detection. The precision and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were also acceptable Significantly, the practicality of this sensitive sensor for OTC detection in drugs was further validated, revealing the advantages of high simplicity, sensitivity and selectivity. Hence, the developed biosensor might provide a useful and practical tool for OTC determination and related food safety analysis and clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence detection
Inner filter effect
Carbon quantum dots
Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles
Oxytetracyline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble CDs using the hydrothermal method
Characterization of CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs
Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of OTC, Fe3O4 MNPs and H2O2
Detections of TC in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the OTC assay based on the fluorescence quenching method.
(A) AFM image of CDs, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence emission spectra (b) of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different conditions. The concentrations of Fe3O4 MNPs, OTC and H2O2 were 0.7mgmL−1, 2.5μM and 50mM, respectively. Buffer: 0.1M NaAc-HAc (pH 4.0), incubation time: 0min, reaction time: 30min, reaction temperature: 57°C.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of OTC. (B) Calibration curve for OTC detection. Inset shows the linear responses at low OTC concentrations. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.

The change of fluorescence intensity (A) and fluorescence intensity decrease ((F0-F)/F0) (B) of CDs in the presence of OTC (1.25μM), doxycycline (2.5μM), TC (2.5μM), penicillin (25μM), ribostamycin sulfate salt (25μM), streptomycin sulfate (25μM), kanamycin sulfate (25μM), naringin (25μM), hesperidin (25μM) and ononin (25μM). Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.
Comparisons of the linear range and detection limit of different methods in the detection of OTC.
Results of OTC determination and recoveries in OTC tablets.
Recoveries of OTC in supplemented milk detected by current method.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive fluorometric determination of oxytetracycline based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 MNPs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Siping 136000, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, selective and eco-friendly biosensor for the determination of tetracyclines based on the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and carbon dots are presented.

In this paper, a novel, selective and eco-friendly biosensor has been fabricated and applied for sensitive detection of antibiotic oxytetracycline (OTC). This fluorescent biosensor was based on carbon quantum dots (CDs), H2O2 and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs). Herein, we firstly demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of OTC, H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs. The fluorescence quenching of CDs not only is caused by the hydroxyl radical produced from the Fenton reaction and complexation of Fe3O4 MNPs with OTC, but also might be related to the inner filter effect (IFE) between CDs and OTC, which improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the fabricated biosensor. Under the optimal experiment conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased linearly with the increasing concentration of OTC from 25nM to 1.75μM. The detection limit of the fabricated biosensor on OTC was 9.5nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Compared with other current protocols, the method described here displayed a few advantages including simplicity, low cost and rapid detection. The precision and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were also acceptable Significantly, the practicality of this sensitive sensor for OTC detection in drugs was further validated, revealing the advantages of high simplicity, sensitivity and selectivity. Hence, the developed biosensor might provide a useful and practical tool for OTC determination and related food safety analysis and clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), a tetracycline derivative, is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent. Based on its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, favorable oral absorption, relatively less toxicity and low cost, OTC has been extensively used in human medicine, eterinary medicine, fruits and vegetables for the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections or as a feed additive to promote the growth of livestock [1]. However, the irrational abusing of OTC, as well as the improper disposal of residues, could cause accumulation of antibiotics in animal products, including meat, milk and honey [2]. As a result, humans have an increased chance of a long term intake of OTC, which may affect the growth and formation of teeth or induce an allergic reaction in the human body [3]. More worse, the residues may also promote bacterial resistance to antibiotics [4]. Therefore, several countries have set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for many food products and WHO has announced a maximum allowable level of 0.1mgL−1 in drugs and human food [5]. Thus, it is urgently needed to provide a facile, rapid and effective approach to detect OTC in contaminated food products.

Up to now, various analytical methods have been developed for the determination of tetracyclines (TCs), especially traditional techniques such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) [7], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [8], and enzyme-linked aptamer assay [9], etc. Generally, HPLC/LC–MS technique shows high sensitivity and identification ability for targeting analytes, but the requirement of sophisticated instrumentation limits its application in routine analysis. Enzyme-linked aptamer assay is also not suitable due to the drawbacks of uneconomical and complicated sample preparation. Consequently, the simple, economical, and efficient methods for detections of TCs remain necessary [10]. Recently, fluorescent detection has become an alternative approach in analytical fields, owing to its advantages of nondestructive nature, operational convenience, low cost and high sensitivity [11,12]. Therefore, we propose a fluorescent assay for OTC detection.

The inner filter effect (IFE), one of the non-irradiation energy conversions, results from the absorption of the excitation or emission light of luminescent species by absorbent, wherein the absorption spectrum of the absorbers should possess a complementary overlap region with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorescent dye [13]. It is worth noting that an IFE system does not require any covalent interactions between the fluorophore and the quencher. Therefore, it has been widely employed for the design and development of novel probes for highly sensitive fluorescent assays [14,15]. Recently, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) have elicited much research interest due to their exceptional advantages over conventional semiconductor quantum dots, such as easy preparation, bright fluorescence, good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, favorable biocompatibility and convenient surface functionalization [16]. Moreover, CDs are suitable electron donors or acceptors. These merits make CDs especially useful for fluorescent bioimaging and biosensing [17]. As a consequence, the fascinating CDs have been explored as fluorescent probes for environmental or biological detection [18].

Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 MNPs) are attractive materials which are particularly useful for drug delivery [19,20] and separation techniques [21,22] due to their basic magnetic property. Unexpectedly, a landmark work by Yan and coworkers reported that Fe3O4 MNPs possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [23], which is similar to the catalysis of natural peroxidase enzyme such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP). In comparison with HRP, Fe3O4 MNPs are readily prepared, more robust, cost effective and easier to be separated from the product for recycling [24,25]. However, so far there are only a few papers reporting on the employing peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4 MNPs in CDs-based fluorescence biosensor.

In our previous work, experiments results showed that the complexation of Fe3O4 MNPs and TCs can lead to accelerated catalyzing H2O2-mediated oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by Fenton chemistry [26]. The reaction starts with oxidation of Fe3O4 MNPs-TCs complex by dissolved oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), primarily ·OH. Herein, as a continuation of our previous work, we designed a new CDs-based fluorometric method with Fe3O4 MNPs as peroxidase mimetic catalyst. The fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by ·OH more quickly and efficiently, meanwhile, the absorption bands of OTC possess a complementary overlap region with the excitation band of CDs, leading to the fluorescence of CDs quenched by IFE between OTC and CDs.

Based on this principle, a simple, eco-friendly and sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of OTC was constructed. CDs were utilized as a fluorescence probe for the detection of OTC in the presence of Fe3O4 MNPs and H2O2. It was found that the fluorescence of CDs was quenched linearly by OTC over the certain concentration range from 25nM to 1.75μM. The detection limit of OTC was 9.5nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The sensor showed a wide linear detection range, good selectivity and low detection limit.

OTC, tetracycline (TC), kanamycin sulfate, and ethylenediamine monohydrate were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Doxycycline hydrochloride and ribostamycin sulfate salt Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Penicillin and streptomycin sulfate were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Naringin, hesperidin and ononin were purchased from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), citric acid monohydrate, ethylene glycol, sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), acetic acid glacial (HAc) and H2O2 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). All of these reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further treatment. OTC tablets were purchased randomly from a drugstore in Changchun, China. Acetate buffer solutions with different pH values were prepared by mixing the stock solution of 0.1M acetate acid and 0.1M sodium acetate. Ultrapure water produced by a Milli-Q system was used throughout this work. All glassware were thoroughly cleaned with aqua regia and rewashed with ultrapure water.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments were performed on a TEM-1011 (JEOL Co., Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 100kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) experiments were carried out with a Nanoscope Multimode VIII instrument in scanasyst mode. The AFM specimen was prepared by placing 10 μL of the purified CDs solution on the surface of newly-peeled mica and was dried at room temperature. The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded using a CARY500 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The solution for the fluorescence measurement is put in a quartz fluorescence cuvette with 1cm optical path length, while the emission slit is 5nm and the exciting slit is 10nm. The emission spectra excited at 360nm are detected in the wavelength of 400–550nm.

Fe3O4 MNPs were prepared with a solvothermal reduction method according to the literature with minor modification [27]. In brief, FeCl3·6H2O (0.68g, 2.5mmol) was dissolved in ethylene glycol (20mL) to form a clear solution, followed by addition of NaAc (1.8g, 21.9mmol). The mixture was stirred vigorously for 30min and then sealed in a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (40mL capacity). The autoclave was heated and maintained at 200°C for 8h, and allowed to cool to room temperature. The black products were collected by an external permanent magnet, washed several times with ethanol. The obtained products freeze-dried in the dark for 48h. The as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs were dispersed in 0.1M acetate buffer (pH 4.0) and subjected to ultrasound for 1min to make a 5mg/mL stock solution and stored at 4°C in dark before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble CDs using the hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble CDs are prepared using the hydrothermal method according to the literature with minor modification [28]. Briefly, 0.42g citric acid and 0.536mL ethylenediamine is dissolved in 10mL water and loaded into a 30mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was put into a 200°C drying oven and kept for 5h. The product can be used after being filtered through a filtration membrane (0.22μm).

The procedure for OTC detection is described as follows: 10μL of 0.7mgmL−1 Fe3O4 MNPs colloidal solution and 10μL of OTC solutions with different concentrations were added into 320μL of 0.1M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Subsequently, 50μL of diluted CDs and 10μL of H2O2 (10mM) were added into the solution. The mixture was incubated in a water bath at 57°C for 30min. The fluorescence emissions from 400nm to 550nm upon excitation of the light of 360nm were measured, defined as F. Meanwhile, a reagent blank (F0) was recorded, and a value of ΔF=(F0−F)/F0 was calculated. Other antibiotics such as TC, doxycycline, ribostamycin, penicillin and streptomycin were examined under the same procedure for the specificity tests.

For the OTC tablets, before grinding, ten pieces of OTC tablets were weighted, and then the average amount of one tablet was calculated. The labeled amount of every tablet is 125mg. The weight of ten pieces of OTC tablets is 2.0692g, and then the average amount of one tablet was 206.9mg. The molar mass of OTC is 496.89gmol−1. The required amount of OTC (1mM, 5mL) is 2.48mg. Calculated amount=
a v e r a g e a m o u n t
l a b e l e d a m o u n t
× r e q u i r e d a m o u n t
=4.11mg. Therefore, after grinding to fine powder, the amount equal to 4.11mg was weighted accurately, and then transferred into a 7mL centrifuge tube. After that, 5mL ultrapure water was added into the tube to dissolve the powder. The samples were diluted 50 times with ultrapure water when in use.

Milk samples were purchased from local super-markets. To remove the proteins, milk samples were mixed with 2M trichloroacetic acid solution and then sonicated for 15min. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min. After the pH value of the supernatant was adjusted to neutrality with NaOH, the solution was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane. Finally, the filtrate was collected into 3 tubes and supplemented with OTC solutions (0.25μM, 0.75μM, 1.25μM). All the spiked samples described here were further subjected to the detection procedure.
All of the samples were stored at 4°C away from light before fluorescent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and Fe3O4 MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CDs were characterized by AFM, fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs were mono-dispersed with a typical topographic height of 1nm, which could be measured from the height profile. To evaluate the optical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1B (a)) and fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 1B (b)) were investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs shows an absorption peak at 360nm. The PL spectrum of CDs showed a 448nm emission peak under the excitation of 360nm.

The morphology of Fe3O4 MNPs was confirmed by TEM characterization. As shown in Fig. S1, the resultant Fe3O4 MNPs through solvothermal reduction of FeCl3 were roughly spherical in shape and the diameter distributed in the range of 350–450nm.
Scheme 1 illustrates the design principle of the fluorescence sensor for the detection of OTC.

It is well known that Fe2+ reacts with H2O2 to yield the hydroxyl radical in sulfuric acid, which is called as the Fenton reaction [29,30]. Peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4 MNPs stems from its structural similarity to the Fenton’s reagent (Fe2+/Fe3+ ions in solution) [31,32]. When excessive H2O2 were added into the reaction solution, a redox process Fe2+ ↔Fe3+ on MNPs surface took place to produce hydroxyl (·OH). The hydroxyl radicals cause the fluorescence quenching of CDs (Scheme 1(a)) [33]. Recently, Huang et al. showed that OTC have a strong tendency to complex with metal ions such as Fe(II) and Fe(III) in aquatic environments, and in another case the complexation of Fe(II) with OTC could generate reactive oxygen species (primarily ·OH) in the presence of dissolved oxygen (Scheme 1(b)) [34]. In addition, there is a good spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the OTC and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs (Fig. S2), and the corresponding FL spectra show that the addition of OTC decreases the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, but has no effect on the fluorescence wavelength (Fig. 2 (B)), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching might be related to IFE (Scheme 1(c)) [35–37].

To rule out the possibility that the fluorescence quenching of CDs is caused by leaching of iron ions into acidic solution, we incubated 0.7mgmL−1 Fe3O4 MNPs in the standard reaction buffer (pH 4.0) for 10min and then removed the MNPs from solution with a magnet. And then, we compared the activity of the leaching solution (ΔF2) with that of Fe3O4 MNPs (ΔF2) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S3 the value of ΔF2 is always lower than ΔF1. The results suggest that leaching Fe(II) ions could react near CDs surface with adsorbed OTC according to known Fenton reaction, but the presence of ferrous and ferric ions on the surface of Fe3O4 MNPs is likely to be the key to their catalysis [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of OTC, Fe3O4 MNPs and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: We measured the change of the fluorescence intensity for CDs solution in the presence of OTC, H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs, respectively (Fig. 2). The fluorescence intensity of CDs solution containing H2O2, OTC or Fe3O4 MNPs at 448nm changes inconspicuously. However, when OTC, H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs were simultaneously added into the CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased apparently. According to the above results, we propose that OTC, H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs system show a better performance for quenching CDs compared to that of the three components alone or duplex/hybrid complexes.

Many previous reports showed that Fe3O4 NPs can effectively adsorb TC through formation of stable metal-ligand complex, and surface complexation appeared to be the dominant mechanism of oxytetracycline sorption to iron oxides [38,39]. We also examined the complexation of OTCs with Fe3O4 by UV absorbance spectra as the literature reported [40]. UV–vis absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. S4. The experimental results show that, in the presence of MNPs, the absorbance increased significantly at around 280–315nm, which implied that the MNPs-OTC complexation formed. In contrast, the absorbance at around 340–370nm was lower. Thus, the results in Fig. S4 suggest that MNPs likely complexes with OTC.

In this work, we observed that the addition of OTC can remarkably enhance the fluorescence quenching effect of H2O2 and Fe3O4 MNPs on CDs, thus permitting the development of a label-free fluorescence detection of OTC. Based on this principle, we achieved the detection of OTC in different samples with high selectivity and sensitivity. The present fluorescence TCs assay is simple, inexpensive and efficient without requirement of chemical labelling. Moreover, this method has been applied for the determination of OTC content in drugs and milk with satisfactory results.

A series of experiments were carried out towards the purpose of getting the best performance of sensor. We used ΔF as a signal for the detection of OTC, where ΔF=(F0 −F)/F0. As the pH may play a critical role to obtain the maximal decrease of the fluorescence intensity, we firstly investigated the influence of pH on ΔF values (Fig. S5). The pH values chosen in this study were 3.6, 4.0, 4.4, 4.8 and 5.2. The ΔF reached the maximum as well as pH 4.0. Then, we investigated the influence of the concentration of Fe3O4 MNPs on ΔF (Fig. S6). Different concentrations of Fe3O4 MNPs (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, and 1.0mgmL−1) were selected. The ΔF increased to the maximum value with the increasing of Fe3O4 MNPs concentration to 0.7mgmL−1. However, the ΔF decreased when the concentration of Fe3O4 MNPs was further increased. As a result, the concentration of Fe3O4 MNPs was determined as 0.7mgmL−1. Likewise, the influence of different concentrations of H2O2 (5, 10, 30, 50, 70, and 100mM) has been also studied. It can be found that the ΔF decreased when the concentration of H2O2 was further increased (Fig. S7), so the concentration of H2O2 was decided as 10mM.

Considering the possible influence of incubation temperature and time on the detections, experiments towards temperature and corresponding time were investigated successively. In brief, varying time (0, 3, 5, 7, 10min) for reacting between Fe3O4 MNPs and OTC or H2O2 has been investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. ΔF changes inconspicuously when increasing incubation time from 0 to 10min. Therefore, we speculated that the complex formed by Fe3O4 MNPs coordinating with OTC can be formed immediately. Furthermore, as described in Fig. S10, the ΔF reached a plateau at 30min. In Fig. S11, ΔF increased gradually with reaction temperature varying from 37°C to 57°C, and changed slightly as well as the reaction temperature exceeded 57°C, demonstrating that ΔF were also indeed dependent on the reaction temperature. Eventually, these parameters of pH 4.0, 0.7mgmL−1 Fe3O4 MNPs, 10mM H2O2, 57°C, and 30min were set as the optimal conditions for sensing OTC during all the following experiment procedures.

After experimental conditions are optimized, the linear response range of the sensing system was measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensities of the CDs decrease according to the concentration of the OTC added. The results exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9908) in the range of 25 nM-1.75μM between ΔF and the concentration of OTC with the following equation: ΔF=0.0538+0.1594c (where c is the concentration of OTC), demonstrating the satisfactory precision of this fluorescent assay. Correspondingly, the detection limit (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) for OTC detection is as low as 9.5nM, which can be compared favorably with those of previous reports of OTC detection (Table 1 ). The reproducibility of the proposed method is evaluated by six repeated measurements of 1.25μM OTC, and the relative standard deviation is 3.3%, proving the reliability of the detection method. Therefore, the results obtained here have proved it is a promising method for assaying OTC.

We investigated the selectivity of the current method. The study on the anti-interference ability was carried out using buffer solution with TC (2.5μM), doxycycline (2.5μM), kanamycin sulfate (25μM), streptomycin (25μM), penicillin (25μM), ribostamycin (25μM), naringin (25μM), hesperidin (25μM) and ononin (25μM) in place of OTC (1.25μM) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , the ΔF caused by other antibiotics or flavonoids was negligible compared with that of OTC. The results proved that the proposed biosensor has high selectivity towards OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of TC in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a novel and well-designed turn-off sensing strategy has been proposed for OTC assay, by using CDs-Fe3O4 MNPs-H2O2 assembly as fluorescence reporters. The detection of OTC was based on the fluorescent decrease of CDs, and the quenching of CDs not only is caused by the hydroxyl radical produced from the Fenton reaction and complexation of Fe3O4 MNPs with OTC, but also might be related to the inner filter effect (IFE) between the CDs and OTC, which improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. The as-prepared CDs-Fe3O4 MNPs-H2O2 composites were applied to the trace detection of OTC successfully. This eco-friendly optical sensor demonstrated a lower detection limit and good accuracy in complex samples analysis, which reveals great potential in advanced analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405146, 21275135) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFC1400601) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.182.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yilin Wang obtained her B.Sc. degree in 2012 from Zhengzhou University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Pengjuan Ni obtained her B.Sc. degree in 2011 from Hebei University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Shu Jiang obtained her B.Sc. degree in 2011 from Hunan Normal University. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Wangdong Lu obtained her M.Sc. degree in 2013 from Northeast Normal University. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Zhen Li obtained his B.Sc. degree in 2012 from Liaocheng University, China. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Hanmeng Liu obtained her B.Sc. degree in 2015 from Yanbian University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Jing Lin obtained her B.Sc. degree in 2015 from Baicheng Normal University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her M.S. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Yujing Sun received her Ph.D. degree in 2010 from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and her supervisor is Prof. Zhuang Li. At present, she works as a Research Associate in Zhuang Li’s group at the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Zhuang Li is a Professor of chemistry at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. Professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences since 1998. His research interests include scanning probe microscopy; visioning DNA/protein and their interaction; nanomanipulations and nanodevices; ultrafast and accurate detection of trace chemical substances.
